rideshare-accidents.q: Whose insurance pays if I'm hurt in an Uber or Lyft accident?
rideshare-accidents.a: It depends on which 'period' the rideshare driver was in: offline (personal insurance), waiting for a request (limited rideshare coverage), or actively driving a passenger (the rideshare company's full $1M policy in most states). An attorney can identify the right coverage for your situation.

rideshare-accidents.q: I was a passenger in the Uber — do I have a case?
rideshare-accidents.a: Yes. As a passenger you are almost never at fault, and Uber and Lyft both carry up to $1 million in liability coverage that applies to passengers during a trip. We connect you with attorneys who handle these claims regularly.

rideshare-accidents.q: How long do I have to file a rideshare accident claim?
rideshare-accidents.a: The deadline (statute of limitations) depends on your state — typically two to four years from the date of the accident. Don't wait; evidence and witness recollection fade fast.

rideshare-accidents.q: Does it cost anything to talk to an attorney?
rideshare-accidents.a: No. The attorneys in our network work on contingency — you pay nothing unless they recover compensation for you. The case evaluation is free.

pedestrian-accidents.q: What if the driver who hit me drove off?
pedestrian-accidents.a: If the at-fault driver fled the scene, your own auto insurance may cover you under uninsured-motorist coverage even though you were on foot. An attorney can identify every available source of recovery.

pedestrian-accidents.q: I was crossing outside the crosswalk — can I still recover?
pedestrian-accidents.a: Often yes. Most states use comparative fault, meaning you can still recover damages even if you were partly at fault. The driver's negligence still matters.

pedestrian-accidents.q: How long do pedestrian accident cases take to settle?
pedestrian-accidents.a: Simple cases can resolve in a few months; cases involving serious injury or disputed liability can take a year or more. Your attorney will give you a realistic timeline based on your specific facts.
Medical bills (current and future), lost wages, pain and suffering, and in tragic cases, wrongful-death damages for surviving family members.

motorcycle-injury.q: Insurance is blaming me because I was on a motorcycle. What now?
motorcycle-injury.a: Insurance companies often try to shift blame to riders. An experienced motorcycle injury attorney pushes back with accident reconstruction, witness testimony, and evidence that establishes the actual fault.

motorcycle-injury.q: Does not wearing a helmet hurt my case?
motorcycle-injury.a: It depends on the state and the injury. Even in helmet-required states, lack of a helmet usually only affects head-injury damages, not damages for other injuries the helmet wouldn't have prevented.

motorcycle-injury.q: Can I recover for road rash and scarring?
motorcycle-injury.a: Yes. Road rash often leaves permanent scarring, and scarring damages are recoverable as part of pain and suffering.

motorcycle-injury.q: What if the other driver had no insurance?
motorcycle-injury.a: Your own uninsured/underinsured-motorist coverage typically applies. We connect you with attorneys who know how to maximize that recovery.

commercial-vehicles.q: Why are truck accident cases different from regular car accidents?
commercial-vehicles.a: Trucking cases involve federal regulations (FMCSA hours-of-service, maintenance, drug testing), corporate defendants with deep-pocket policies, and time-sensitive evidence like ELD logs that can be overwritten. The right attorney preserves that evidence immediately.

commercial-vehicles.q: Who can be held responsible besides the driver?
commercial-vehicles.a: Often the trucking company, its broker, the cargo loader, the maintenance contractor, or even the truck manufacturer if equipment failed. Multiple defendants usually mean more available coverage.

commercial-vehicles.q: How long do I have to act?
commercial-vehicles.a: Federal preservation letters should go out fast — within days. The legal filing deadline (statute of limitations) is generally two to four years depending on your state.

commercial-vehicles.q: What if my injuries are severe or permanent?
commercial-vehicles.a: Catastrophic-injury truck cases routinely settle in the high six or seven figures. Your attorney builds the medical and economic record needed to support that level of recovery.
